(**WNY**) is the westernmost region of the [U.S. state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") of [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)"). The eastern boundary of the region is not consistently defined by state agencies or those who call themselves "Western New Yorkers". Almost all sources agree WNY includes the cities of [Buffalo](/wiki/Buffalo,_New_York "Buffalo, New York"), [Niagara Falls](/wiki/Niagara_Falls,_New_York "Niagara Falls, New York"), [Jamestown](/wiki/Jamestown,_New_York "Jamestown, New York"), and the surrounding suburbs, as well as the outlying rural areas of [Niagara Frontier](/wiki/Niagara_Frontier "Niagara Frontier"), and Chautauqua-Alleghany (or the western [Southern Tier](/wiki/Southern_Tier "Southern Tier")). Many would also place [Rochester](/wiki/Rochester,_New_York "Rochester, New York") and the [Genesee Valley](/wiki/Genesee_Valley "Genesee Valley") in the region, although these locations are also sometimes included in the [Finger Lakes](/wiki/Finger_Lakes "Finger Lakes") Region.

**Western New York** (**WNY**) is the westernmost region of the [U.S. state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") of [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)"). The eastern boundary of the region is not consistently defined by state agencies or those who call themselves "Western New Yorkers". Almost all sources agree WNY includes the cities of [Buffalo](/wiki/Buffalo,_New_York "Buffalo, New York"), [Niagara Falls](/wiki/Niagara_Falls,_New_York "Niagara Falls, New York"), [Jamestown](/wiki/Jamestown,_New_York "Jamestown, New York"), and the surrounding suburbs, as well as the outlying rural areas of [Niagara Frontier](/wiki/Niagara_Frontier "Niagara Frontier"), and Chautauqua-Alleghany (or the western [Southern Tier](/wiki/Southern_Tier "Southern Tier")). Many would also place [Rochester](/wiki/Rochester,_New_York "Rochester, New York") and the [Genesee Valley](/wiki/Genesee_Valley "Genesee Valley") in the region, although these locations are also sometimes included in the [Finger Lakes](/wiki/Finger_Lakes "Finger Lakes") Region.

The State of New York sometimes defines the WNY region as including just five counties: [Allegany](/wiki/Allegany_County,_New_York "Allegany County, New York"), [Cattaraugus](/wiki/Cattaraugus_County,_New_York "Cattaraugus County, New York"), [Chautauqua](/wiki/Chautauqua_County,_New_York "Chautauqua County, New York"), [Erie](/wiki/Erie_County,_New_York "Erie County, New York"), and [Niagara](/wiki/Niagara_County,_New_York "Niagara County, New York"). The state's Empire State Development Corporation and state health authorities have both mapped the region this way.[[1]](#cite_note-1) The state has also used this regional boundary to set policy during the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_New_York_(state) "COVID-19 pandemic in New York (state)").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

More commonly, WNY has been defined to include at least [Genesee](/wiki/Genesee_County,_New_York "Genesee County, New York"), [Orleans](/wiki/Orleans_County,_New_York "Orleans County, New York"), and [Wyoming](/wiki/Wyoming_County,_New_York "Wyoming County, New York") counties as well. Almost all descriptions of WNY in the Buffalo media market define it as this eight county area.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Most organizations that use WNY in their name including the WNY College Consortium, the WNY Genealogical Society, WNY PRISM, the Psychological Association of WNY, and the WNY Land Conservancy describe themselves as working in these eight counties.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)

Others, such as Clarion Publications, the publisher of the WNY Travel Guide and WesternNY.com, have used the term Western New York to describe a much larger area consisting of 17 counties. In addition to those counties mentioned, they add [Chemung](/wiki/Chemung_County,_New_York "Chemung County, New York"), [Livingston](/wiki/Livingston_County,_New_York "Livingston County, New York"), [Monroe](/wiki/Monroe_County,_New_York "Monroe County, New York"), [Ontario](/wiki/Ontario_County,_New_York "Ontario County, New York"), [Schuyler](/wiki/Schuyler_County,_New_York "Schuyler County, New York"), [Seneca](/wiki/Seneca_County,_New_York "Seneca County, New York"), [Steuben](/wiki/Steuben_County,_New_York "Steuben County, New York"), [Wayne](/wiki/Wayne_County,_New_York "Wayne County, New York"), and [Yates](/wiki/Yates_County,_New_York "Yates County, New York") as well.[[9]](#cite_note-:0-9)

The rest of this article uses that definition. The 17 counties have a land area of 11,764 square miles (30,470 km2).[[9]](#cite_note-:0-9) They include the area of the [Holland Purchase](/wiki/Holland_Purchase "Holland Purchase") and the [Phelps and Gorham Purchase](/wiki/Phelps_and_Gorham_Purchase "Phelps and Gorham Purchase").

The area is primarily served by Buffalo and Rochester [media markets](/wiki/Media_market "Media market"), although there is considerable overlap between these two markets, as well as other American and Canadian media markets (particularly from nearby [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto "Toronto"))[citation needed]. In terms of the [combined statistical areas](/wiki/Combined_statistical_area "Combined statistical area") used by the [United States Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau"), Western New York consists of the [Buffalo–Cheektowaga–Olean, NY](/wiki/Buffalo-Cheektowaga-Olean,_NY_Combined_Statistical_Area "Buffalo-Cheektowaga-Olean, NY Combined Statistical Area") area, the [Rochester–Batavia–Seneca Falls, NY](/wiki/Rochester-Batavia-Seneca_Falls,_NY_Combined_Statistical_Area "Rochester-Batavia-Seneca Falls, NY Combined Statistical Area") area, and the [Elmira–Corning, NY](/wiki/Elmira-Corning,_NY_Combined_Statistical_Area "Elmira-Corning, NY Combined Statistical Area") area.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Western New York is in some contexts considered a sub-region of "[Upstate New York](/wiki/Upstate_New_York "Upstate New York")" (when that term is used to describe all of New York State outside [Long Island](/wiki/Long_Island "Long Island") and the [New York City metropolitan area](/wiki/New_York_City_metropolitan_area "New York City metropolitan area")); in most cases however, New Yorkers identify by specific regionalities such as Western New York or Central New York in preference to the vague and broader term Upstate New York.

## Subregions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_New_York&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Subregions")]

Western New York is generally considered to have 2–4 subregions:

1. Greater Niagara
2. Chautauqua–Allegheny
3. Genesee
4. Western Finger Lakes (sometimes considered part of Central New York)

The Greater Niagara Region (or the Niagara Frontier), comprises Niagara and Erie counties and parts of Genesee and Wyoming counties. It is named for its major features, the Niagara river and [Niagara Falls](/wiki/Niagara_Falls "Niagara Falls"). [Buffalo](/wiki/Buffalo,_New_York "Buffalo, New York") is its largest urban center.

The Chautauqua–Allegheny Region (also known as Southwestern New York or the Western [Southern Tier](/wiki/Southern_Tier "Southern Tier")) comprises Chautauqua, Cattaraugus, and Allegany counties. The region is mountainous. The largest urban center is [Jamestown](/wiki/Jamestown,_New_York "Jamestown, New York"), and [Chautauqua Lake](/wiki/Chautauqua_Lake "Chautauqua Lake") and [Allegany State Park](/wiki/Allegany_State_Park "Allegany State Park") are major geographical features.

The third region, the Genesee Region, comprises Monroe, Livingston, Wayne, Orleans, Ontario, and Yates counties and parts of Genesee and Wyoming counties. [Rochester](/wiki/Rochester,_New_York "Rochester, New York") is its largest urban center. Letchworth State Park is a major geographical feature.

The fourth region is that of the western Finger Lakes Region composed of parts of Seneca, Steuben, Schuyler, and Chemung counties. While parts of the Finger Lakes are considered within Western New York (as either a region or quadrant) the eastern portions are typically considered a part of Central New York.

The Genesee region and Western Finger Lakes region are often combined and described as the [Finger Lakes](/wiki/Finger_Lakes "Finger Lakes") region.

Western New York also includes several [Seneca Nation](/wiki/Seneca_Nation_of_New_York "Seneca Nation of New York") enclaves.

If it were counted as a single area, the population of Western New York would number just over 2.6 million, and would rank as the 24th largest [metropolitan area](/wiki/Metropolitan_area "Metropolitan area") of the United States, between the [Pittsburgh metropolitan area](/wiki/Pittsburgh_metropolitan_area "Pittsburgh metropolitan area") and the [Sacramento, California metropolitan area](/wiki/Sacramento,_California_metropolitan_area "Sacramento, California metropolitan area"). If it were counted as a state, the population of Western New York would rank as the 37th most populated state in the United States.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/LakeEffectSnowBuffalo101206.gif/250px-LakeEffectSnowBuffalo101206.gif)](/wiki/File:LakeEffectSnowBuffalo101206.gif)

[Lake-effect snow](/wiki/Lake-effect_snow "Lake-effect snow") is a major contributor to snowfall totals in western New York

Western New York has a [humid continental climate](/wiki/Humid_continental_climate "Humid continental climate") heavily influenced by both [Lake Erie](/wiki/Lake_Erie "Lake Erie") and [Lake Ontario](/wiki/Lake_Ontario "Lake Ontario"). Winters are long and cold, often lasting from around mid-November to early April. There are often snows before and after that period, as well. Western New York is known for its [lake effect](/wiki/Lake-effect_snow "Lake-effect snow") snows, which can result in highly localized, sometimes intense and even historic snow events. Lake effect storms are a result of cold air picking up water vapor as it blows over warm lake waters. Lake effect snows are usually most active between November and February and typically diminish when Lake Erie freezes over. Generally, the heaviest amount of snow in Western New York during the winter falls near the southern end of Erie County as well as in Cattaraugus, Chautauqua, and Wyoming counties. Spring and fall in Western New York are usually short and changeable. The presence of the lakes allows for fruit growing and wine production along areas adjacent to both lakes, which retard the development of damaging spring and fall frost, thereby extending the growing season. Lost in its famed winters, Western New York summers are among the sunniest in the Northeast and are generally very pleasant. Thanks in part to breezes blowing over Lakes Erie and Ontario (which are usually cooler than the air temperature in the summer), most of Western New York enjoy generally cooler and more comfortable summers than other regions in the same climatic zone.

Western New York has two airports that provide significant regular passenger service, [Buffalo–Niagara International Airport](/wiki/Buffalo_Niagara_International_Airport "Buffalo Niagara International Airport") and [Greater Rochester International Airport](/wiki/Greater_Rochester_International_Airport "Greater Rochester International Airport").[[18]](#cite_note-22)[[19]](#cite_note-23) Buffalo–Niagara International Airport is the most patronized airport facility in Western New York. While it primarily serves as the regional airport for the [Buffalo–Niagara Falls Metropolitan Area](/wiki/Buffalo%E2%80%93Niagara_Falls_metropolitan_area "Buffalo–Niagara Falls metropolitan area"), the facility doubles as a gateway to Canada, and a good portion of its passengers (33%) are Canadian. The second major airport in Western New York, [Greater Rochester International Airport](/wiki/Greater_Rochester_International_Airport "Greater Rochester International Airport"), does not see as much traffic as Buffalo–Niagara International Airport. Still, located just three nautical miles southwest of Downtown Rochester, the facility provides convenient access to airline service for many residents of the [Rochester Metropolitan Area](/wiki/Rochester,_New_York_metropolitan_area "Rochester, New York metropolitan area").

The northern part of Western New York features four railroad stations in service on the [Empire Corridor](/wiki/Empire_Corridor "Empire Corridor"): [Rochester](/wiki/Louise_M._Slaughter_Rochester_Station "Louise M. Slaughter Rochester Station"), [Buffalo–Depew](/wiki/Buffalo%E2%80%93Depew_station "Buffalo–Depew station"), [Buffalo–Exchange Street](/wiki/Buffalo%E2%80%93Exchange_Street_station "Buffalo–Exchange Street station") and [Niagara Falls](/wiki/Niagara_Falls,_New_York_(Amtrak_station) "Niagara Falls, New York (Amtrak station)").[[20]](#cite_note-24) The Buffalo–Exchange Street and Niagara Falls stations do not see as much rail service as the other two Western New York stations due to the fact that, west of Depew Station, Amtrak's [Lake Shore Limited](/wiki/Lake_Shore_Limited "Lake Shore Limited") leaves the Empire Corridor en route to the Midwest. For a period of time, there were proposals to service these four stations with [high-speed rail](/wiki/New_York_high-speed_rail "New York high-speed rail"). A major objective of implementing high-speed rail service was to better connect Western New York as well as the rest of Upstate New York with New York City. However, little of substance has come of these proposals.

Service to other parts of Western New York ended from the mid-1960s to 1970. The [Erie Lackawanna](/wiki/Erie_Lackawanna "Erie Lackawanna")'s *[Phoebe Snow](/wiki/Phoebe_Snow_(train) "Phoebe Snow (train)")* served on a southeastern trajectory from Buffalo to [Elmira](/wiki/Elmira,_New_York "Elmira, New York"), [Binghamton](/wiki/Binghamton,_New_York "Binghamton, New York"), [Scranton](/wiki/Scranton "Scranton") and [Hoboken](/wiki/Hoboken,_New_Jersey "Hoboken, New Jersey"); service ended in 1966. The E-L's *[Lake Cities](/wiki/Lake_Cities_(Erie_Railroad_train) "Lake Cities (Erie Railroad train)")* originated in [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago"), passing through Western New York's [Jamestown](/wiki/Jamestown,_New_York "Jamestown, New York"), then through the rest of the [Southern Tier](/wiki/Southern_Tier "Southern Tier"), [Orange County](/wiki/Orange_County,_New_York "Orange County, New York") and terminating at Hoboken, NJ; service ended on January 6, 1970. The [Pennsylvania Railroad](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Railroad "Pennsylvania Railroad")'s *Buffalo Day Express* was another route serving Western New York, traveling south to [Baltimore](/wiki/Baltimore "Baltimore") via [Olean](/wiki/Olean,_New_York "Olean, New York") and [Harrisburg](/wiki/Harrisburg "Harrisburg"); this train was discontinued in 1967, but an unnamed Penn Central (PC) successor lasted through April 30, 1971, when AMTRAK assumed most of the remaining U.S. rail passenger service but declined to serve the Baltimore-Harrisburg-Buffalo route of the former PRR/PC.[[21]](#cite_note-25)

The [Buffalo and Pittsburgh Railroad](/wiki/Buffalo_and_Pittsburgh_Railroad "Buffalo and Pittsburgh Railroad") and [WNY&P](/wiki/Western_New_York_and_Pennsylvania_Railroad_(2001) "Western New York and Pennsylvania Railroad (2001)") both operate freight service in the region. A few shortline railroads also offer rail service, including the [New York and Lake Erie Railroad](/wiki/New_York_and_Lake_Erie_Railroad "New York and Lake Erie Railroad") and the [Arcade and Attica Railroad](/wiki/Arcade_and_Attica_Railroad "Arcade and Attica Railroad").

The [Niagara Frontier Transportation Authority](/wiki/Niagara_Frontier_Transportation_Authority "Niagara Frontier Transportation Authority") runs regular bus service throughout the Buffalo–Niagara Falls metropolitan area.[[22]](#cite_note-26) The [Rochester-Genesee Regional Transportation Authority](/wiki/Rochester-Genesee_Regional_Transportation_Authority "Rochester-Genesee Regional Transportation Authority") serves Rochester and surrounding counties, and the [Chautauqua Area Regional Transit System](/wiki/Chautauqua_Area_Regional_Transit_System "Chautauqua Area Regional Transit System") serves Chautauqua County. [First Transit](/wiki/First_Transit "First Transit") contracts with the [Seneca Nation of Indians](/wiki/Seneca_Nation_of_Indians "Seneca Nation of Indians"), the Olean Area Transit System and Access Allegany to provide public transportation to most of the Southern Tier.

In addition to the public transportation authorities, which provide limited intercity connections, several commercial intercity bus services also operate. [Coach USA](/wiki/Coach_USA "Coach USA") operates a loop service between Buffalo, Jamestown and Olean; the bus connects to a route to New York City in Olean and to [Megabus](/wiki/Megabus_(North_America) "Megabus (North America)") service to Toronto and New York City in Buffalo. Fullington [Trailways](/wiki/Trailways "Trailways") operates a daily rural route between Buffalo and various locations in Pennsylvania; [New York Trailways](/wiki/New_York_Trailways "New York Trailways") also serves Buffalo and Rochester, connecting to various locations, as does [Greyhound Bus Lines](/wiki/Greyhound_Bus_Lines "Greyhound Bus Lines"). One discount "[Chinatown service](/wiki/Chinatown_bus_lines "Chinatown bus lines")" operates in Western New York; Ocean Travel runs an [overnight express service](/wiki/Red-eye_flight "Red-eye flight") between Buffalo, Rochester and New York City.

In its early history, Western New York was settled by native cultures including the Hopewell. The territory of Western New York, until the 17th century, was held by the [Neutral Nation](/wiki/Neutral_Nation "Neutral Nation") in the northern Niagara region and the [Wenrohronon](/wiki/Wenrohronon "Wenrohronon") and [Erie Indians](/wiki/Erie_(tribe) "Erie (tribe)") around the [Allegheny River](/wiki/Allegheny_River "Allegheny River"). The [Seneca nation](/wiki/Seneca_nation "Seneca nation") and their allies in the [Iroquois Confederacy](/wiki/Iroquois_Confederacy "Iroquois Confederacy") eliminated those tribes in wars during the [Beaver Wars](/wiki/Beaver_Wars "Beaver Wars") between 1638 and 1701, with any survivors being assimilated into the Senecas (in the case of the Erie and Neutral) or Huron (in the case of the Wenro). The Neutral territory is currently held by [Tuscaroras](/wiki/Tuscarora_(tribe) "Tuscarora (tribe)"), who moved up from [the Carolinas](/wiki/The_Carolinas "The Carolinas") while refugees from the Erie tribes moved south to the Carolinas.

French forces settled in parts of what is now Western New York through much of the 17th century, beginning with [Étienne Brûlé](/wiki/%C3%89tienne_Br%C3%BBl%C3%A9 "Étienne Brûlé")'s pass-through of the region in 1615 and [Joseph de La Roche Daillon](/wiki/Joseph_de_La_Roche_Daillon "Joseph de La Roche Daillon")'s missionary journeys in the 1620s; some relics of their presence, including [Fort Niagara](/wiki/Fort_Niagara "Fort Niagara") and a [portage road](/wiki/Old_Portage_Road "Old Portage Road") that follows modern [New York State Route 394](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_394 "New York State Route 394") between [Lake Erie](/wiki/Lake_Erie "Lake Erie") and [Chautauqua Lake](/wiki/Chautauqua_Lake "Chautauqua Lake"), are still visible in the region.

Western New York's land was acquired from the Iroquois through the [Nanfan Treaty](/wiki/Nanfan_Treaty "Nanfan Treaty"), which ceded the territory to England at the end of the Beaver Wars in 1701. At the time, four of the British colonies ([Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Colony "Pennsylvania Colony"), [New York](/wiki/New_York_Colony "New York Colony"), [Massachusetts](/wiki/Massachusetts_Bay_Colony "Massachusetts Bay Colony") and [Connecticut](/wiki/Connecticut_Colony "Connecticut Colony")) laid claim to the unsettled territory, as did [New France](/wiki/New_France "New France") until the [Seven Years' War](/wiki/Seven_Years%27_War "Seven Years' War"). Most of Western New York was officially off-limits to all of the colonies as a result of the establishment of an [Indian Reserve](/wiki/Indian_Reserve_(1763) "Indian Reserve (1763)") over the area following the Seven Years' War. By the 1780s, only New York and Massachusetts still maintained their claims to the territory, which was separated from mainland New York by a [Preemption Line](/wiki/Preemption_Line "Preemption Line").

Under federal policy designed to extinguish the states' [Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War") debts, the states were required to [extinguish their western land claims](/wiki/State_cessions "State cessions"). Massachusetts extinguished its claim to the territory in exchange for receiving the profits from its sale to settlers. New York was the only colony not to extinguish its western claim, and by way of the [1786 Treaty of Hartford](/wiki/Treaty_of_Hartford_(1786) "Treaty of Hartford (1786)"), the land, with the exception of a [small triangle that was ceded to Pennsylvania in 1792](/wiki/Erie_Triangle "Erie Triangle"), officially became Western New York. (Had New York extinguished its claim, it would have likely become part of the [Northwest Territory](/wiki/Northwest_Territory "Northwest Territory") established the next year and formed part of a new state.) Native title, with the exception of several reservations, was extinguished in the [Treaty of Canandaigua](/wiki/Treaty_of_Canandaigua "Treaty of Canandaigua") in 1794, while the [Phelps and Gorham Purchase](/wiki/Phelps_and_Gorham_Purchase "Phelps and Gorham Purchase") and [Holland Purchase](/wiki/Holland_Purchase "Holland Purchase") opened the territory for settling. During the [War of 1812](/wiki/War_of_1812 "War of 1812") Western New York was part of the [borderland frontier](/wiki/Niagara_Frontier "Niagara Frontier") between the United States and British Canada and became the scene of various military actions.

The two major Western New York cities, Buffalo and Rochester, benefited greatly from the opening of the [Erie Canal](/wiki/Erie_Canal "Erie Canal"). With its strategic position at the western end of the Erie Canal, the eastern end of Lake Erie and proximity to Niagara Falls and Canada, Buffalo emerged as a major port. [Niagara Falls](/wiki/Niagara_Falls "Niagara Falls") provided Buffalo with a ready supply of power, so much so that one of its enduring nicknames is "The City of Light". Buffalo experienced steady growth during the 19th century and at one point was one of the 10 most populated cities in the United States. According to some, Rochester was "America's First Boomtown"[[23]](#cite_note-27) and was a key player in the flour industry (hence its initial nickname "Flour City"). Its growth was attributed to both the completion of the Erie Canal and its resulting significance in the flour industry. Olean was originally planned to be a similar major hub, but it proved to be at a major disadvantage: located near the headwaters of the [Allegheny River](/wiki/Allegheny_River "Allegheny River") (a factor its planners had hoped would make the city a gateway to the Midwest), the river was too shallow to support large boat travel or shipping on a consistent basis.[[24]](#cite_note-28) As a region, Western New York played a significant role in the American economy during the 19th century. Large scale immigration from Ireland, Italy, Poland, Great Britain and Germany soon followed into the region.

The [New Religious Movement](/wiki/New_Religious_Movement "New Religious Movement") known as [Spiritualism](/wiki/Spiritualism_(movement) "Spiritualism (movement)") was among several that arose in the early 19th century [burned-over district](/wiki/Burned-over_district "Burned-over district") of Western New York. Its major center is [Lily Dale](/wiki/Lily_Dale,_New_York "Lily Dale, New York"), one of the largest spiritualism communities in the United States. The original house of the [Fox sisters](/wiki/Fox_sisters "Fox sisters") was relocated to Lily Dale in 1916. [Joseph Smith Jr.](/wiki/Joseph_Smith "Joseph Smith"), founder of the [Latter Day Saint movement](/wiki/Latter_Day_Saint_movement "Latter Day Saint movement"), reported receiving the Book of Mormon in Palmyra, Wayne County. The women's civic organization [Zonta](/wiki/Zonta_International "Zonta International") was founded in Buffalo in 1919.

In the late 20th century this area became part of the [Rust Belt](/wiki/Rust_Belt "Rust Belt") of the United States, being a focal point for the transportation of grain, steel, and coal. The opening of the [Welland Canal](/wiki/Welland_Canal "Welland Canal") in 1957 effectively rendered the narrower Erie Canal obsolete, further exacerbating economic problems in the region. The area saw an economic decline during the period of [deindustrialization](/wiki/Deindustrialization "Deindustrialization"), leaving many empty steel mills including those of [Bethlehem Steel](/wiki/Bethlehem_Steel "Bethlehem Steel"), which was the 2nd largest steel mill in the world. The plant located in [Lackawanna](/wiki/Lackawanna,_New_York "Lackawanna, New York"), New York provided nearly 25,000 jobs to the local economy. [Republic Steel](/wiki/Republic_Steel "Republic Steel") also had a large integrated [steel mill](/wiki/Steel_mill "Steel mill") located in [South Buffalo](/wiki/South_Buffalo "South Buffalo"). [General Motors](/wiki/General_Motors "General Motors") in Tonawanda is the largest vehicle engine plant in the world, which still is a major contributor to the Buffalo area economy. [Ford Motor Company](/wiki/Ford_Motor_Company "Ford Motor Company") also maintains a large manufacturing facility in [Woodlawn](/wiki/Woodlawn,_Erie_County,_New_York "Woodlawn, Erie County, New York"), New York, which is just south of [Buffalo](/wiki/Buffalo,_New_York "Buffalo, New York").

Western New York is culturally a Great Lakes/Interior Northeastern area, with a dose of [Appalachia](/wiki/Appalachia "Appalachia") in the [Southern Tier](/wiki/Southern_Tier "Southern Tier"), an overlapping region of the state. [Buffalo](/wiki/Buffalo,_New_York "Buffalo, New York") appears to have similarities to other Great Lakes cities/areas, while having Northeastern proclivities. The similarities with Chicago and Milwaukee run the list from sharing a common industrial base traditionally built around [steel](/wiki/Steel "Steel") and [automobile](/wiki/Automobile "Automobile") manufacturing. The cities were both developed during the same period in [American history](/wiki/American_history "American history"), so the street patterns, [architecture](/wiki/Architecture "Architecture"), and ethnic communities share a similar appearance. Linguistically, Western New York is part of the [Inland North](/wiki/Inland_Northern_American_English "Inland Northern American English") region of American English, which means it is subject to the [Northern Cities vowel shift](/wiki/Northern_Cities_vowel_shift "Northern Cities vowel shift"); a distinct variant of that accent, "[Buffalo English](/wiki/Buffalo_English "Buffalo English")", is heard in many parts of the region.

Finally, most Western New York sports fans support the [Buffalo Bills](/wiki/Buffalo_Bills "Buffalo Bills") and [Buffalo Sabres](/wiki/Buffalo_Sabres "Buffalo Sabres"). Team loyalties of sports fans in the rest of upstate New York are more divided. Most baseball fans support the [Cleveland Guardians](/wiki/Cleveland_Guardians "Cleveland Guardians"), [New York Yankees](/wiki/New_York_Yankees "New York Yankees") and [New York Mets](/wiki/New_York_Mets "New York Mets"), although the [Toronto Blue Jays](/wiki/Toronto_Blue_Jays "Toronto Blue Jays") have seen an increase in support due to the influx of Canadians in the area - as well as the region's proximity to Toronto, the Blue Jays' increased on-field success in the mid-2010s, the affiliation with the longstanding minor league [Buffalo Bisons](/wiki/Buffalo_Bisons "Buffalo Bisons"), and the Blue Jays playing much of their 2020 and 2021 schedule in Buffalo.[[25]](#cite_note-29)[[26]](#cite_note-30) The [Pittsburgh Pirates](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Pirates "Pittsburgh Pirates") have some following in southwestern New York, and teams with national fanbases such as the [Boston Red Sox](/wiki/Boston_Red_Sox "Boston Red Sox") and [Chicago Cubs](/wiki/Chicago_Cubs "Chicago Cubs") also have followings. The region has no clear favorite professional basketball franchise, with the [Brooklyn Nets](/wiki/Brooklyn_Nets "Brooklyn Nets"), [Cleveland Cavaliers](/wiki/Cleveland_Cavaliers "Cleveland Cavaliers"), [New York Knicks](/wiki/New_York_Knicks "New York Knicks") and [Toronto Raptors](/wiki/Toronto_Raptors "Toronto Raptors") all claiming parts of the region as part of their [regional broadcasting](/wiki/Regional_sports_network "Regional sports network") territories. (Western New York puts more emphasis on [college basketball](/wiki/College_basketball "College basketball"), where the "Big 4" Division I programs—Canisius, Niagara, St. Bonaventure and UB—all have strong followings and rivalries; while most attention has been centered around the men's college basketball teams, the recent success of UB and St. Bonaventure's women's teams has led to increased attention to women's basketball in the region. The college basketball scene once had enough clout to help drive Buffalo's NBA team, the 1970s-era [Buffalo Braves](/wiki/Buffalo_Braves "Buffalo Braves"), out of the city.) Syracuse University sports, being the closest [major-conference school](/wiki/Power_Five_conference "Power Five conference") to the region, also has a sizable following in western New York, particularly in basketball. [College football](/wiki/College_football "College football") interest is minimal, with no team having a major following (not even UB, who has struggled to maintain its Division I football program despite modest success). For hockey, other than the [Buffalo Sabres](/wiki/Buffalo_Sabres "Buffalo Sabres"), the region is also home to a number of [Toronto Maple Leafs](/wiki/Toronto_Maple_Leafs "Toronto Maple Leafs"), [New York Rangers](/wiki/New_York_Rangers "New York Rangers") and [Pittsburgh Penguins](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Penguins "Pittsburgh Penguins") supporters.

## Food

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_New_York&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Food")]

### Region-wide

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_New_York&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Region-wide")]

"Friday night [fish fry](/wiki/Fish_fry "Fish fry")" is popular regionally, a holdover from when Catholics were forbidden to eat meat on Fridays. It is usually skinless cod although most restaurants in the area claim they use the higher quality haddock, and is frequently beer-battered, deep fried, and served with French fries and cole slaw. Numerous restaurants in Western New York have a Friday fish fry special. The fish fry retains particular popularity during [Lent](/wiki/Lent "Lent"), when Catholics are still barred from eating meat on Friday.[[27]](#cite_note-31)

Italian-American food is a specialty in western New York. Most towns in the region have pizzerias.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The [Texas hot](/wiki/Texas_hot "Texas hot"), introduced by Greek immigrants in the 1920s, has a strong following in the region, and several hot dog stands offer the dish. Longstanding Texas hot stands include [Ted's Hot Dogs](/wiki/Ted%27s_Hot_Dogs "Ted's Hot Dogs") in Buffalo (1927), [Rigas](/wiki/John_Rigas "John Rigas") Texas Hot in [Wellsville](/wiki/Wellsville_(village),_New_York "Wellsville (village), New York") (1921),[[28]](#cite_note-32) and Johnny's Lunch in [Jamestown](/wiki/Jamestown,_New_York "Jamestown, New York")[[29]](#cite_note-33)[[30]](#cite_note-34) (1936; the Colera family is also tied to several other Jamestown stands such as AJ Texas Hots, which launched in 1968, and Michael's, which operated from 1976 to 2021).[[31]](#cite_note-35)[[32]](#cite_note-36)

The region produces many agricultural products, including milk, maple syrup, apples, cherries, potatoes, sweet corn, strawberries, raspberries, peaches, and grapes. As with the rest of [Upstate New York](/wiki/Upstate_New_York "Upstate New York"), [dairy farming](/wiki/Dairy_farming "Dairy farming") is an important part of the economy. New York State is a leading producer among the states of dairy, maple, and wine.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Pizza logs](/w/index.php?title=Pizza_log&action=edit&redlink=1 "Pizza log (page does not exist)"), consisting of a deep-fried [egg roll](/wiki/Egg_roll "Egg roll") with pizza filling, originated in and are still manufactured in [Sanborn](/wiki/Sanborn,_New_York "Sanborn, New York").[[33]](#cite_note-37)

### Buffalo area

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_New_York&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Buffalo area")]

Perhaps the best-known food specialty of Western New York is [Buffalo chicken wings](/wiki/Buffalo_chicken_wings "Buffalo chicken wings") (known locally simply as 'wings' or 'chicken wings'). The two best-known places to get wings are [Duff's](/wiki/Duff%27s_Famous_Wings "Duff's Famous Wings") and the [Anchor Bar](/wiki/Anchor_Bar "Anchor Bar"), which have a strong local rivalry in Buffalo and both are expanding outside the city.[[34]](#cite_note-38)

At the 1885 [Erie County Fair](/wiki/Erie_County_Fair "Erie County Fair") (known as the Hamburg Fair) in [Hamburg, New York](/wiki/Hamburg,_New_York "Hamburg, New York"), Frank and Charles Menches sold a ground beef sandwich, which is one of the earliest claims of the invention of the [hamburger](/wiki/Hamburger "Hamburger").[[35]](#cite_note-39)

[Beef on Weck](/wiki/Beef_on_Weck "Beef on Weck") is a regional favorite and a local invention in which sliced warm roast beef is piled high on a [Kummelweck](/wiki/Kummelweck "Kummelweck") roll topped with caraway seeds and large grained salt, *au jus* and horseradish are often added to the sandwich.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Butter lambs](/wiki/Butter_lamb "Butter lamb") are a popular tradition in Buffalo during the Lenten season leading into [Easter](/wiki/Easter_Sunday "Easter Sunday"). The Broadway Market, a market in Buffalo, is famous for this product.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Rochester area

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_New_York&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Rochester area")]

The Rochester area is known for the [Garbage Plate](/wiki/Garbage_Plate "Garbage Plate"). It was created and continues to be served by [Nick Tahou Hots](/wiki/Nick_Tahou_Hots "Nick Tahou Hots") on West Main Street. The name "Garbage Plate" is trademarked by Nick Tahou's, but other Rochester restaurants serve the same or similar dish under other names, usually containing the word "plate". The [Rochester Red Wings](/wiki/Rochester_Red_Wings "Rochester Red Wings") baseball team concessions at [Frontier Field](/wiki/Frontier_Field "Frontier Field"), for example, offer the "Home Plate".

A component of the garbage plate and many other local dishes is a topping known in Rochester as "hot sauce." The term in this context does not refer to a [pepper sauce](/wiki/Pepper_sauce "Pepper sauce"), but to a spiced sauce containing bits of meat.[[36]](#cite_note-40)

Another food associated with the Rochester area is the "[white hot](/wiki/White_hot "White hot")", a [hot dog](/wiki/Hot_dog "Hot dog") with a slight variation of ingredients that appears without the distinctive pink color of most hot dogs; when ordering a hot dog in Rochester it is common to be asked to clarify whether it should be "red or white".[[37]](#cite_note-41)

In urban areas in Western New York, education, business, light manufacturing and tourism have replaced heavy manufacturing which left the area at the end of World War II. Rochester and Buffalo were especially hard-hit with the exodus of manufacturing jobs to other areas of the United States and nations like China and Mexico. [Niagara Falls](/wiki/Niagara_Falls,_New_York "Niagara Falls, New York") was also hard-hit and has shed half of its population since its peak in 1960. Today, major corporations in Western New York include [Kodak](/wiki/Kodak "Kodak"), [Wegmans](/wiki/Wegmans "Wegmans"), [Bausch & Lomb](/wiki/Bausch_%26_Lomb "Bausch & Lomb"), [Xerox](/wiki/Xerox "Xerox"), [M&T Bank](/wiki/M%26T_Bank "M&T Bank"), [KeyBank](/wiki/KeyBank "KeyBank") and [Moog](/wiki/Moog_Inc. "Moog Inc."), among many others. [General Motors](/wiki/General_Motors "General Motors") continues to operate three facilities in the region. Rochester Components Operations, formerly known as Rochester Division, produces fuel system components. Lockport Operations, formerly Harrison Thermal Systems, produces cooling system components and was awarded $154 million for additional and future products in 2022.[[38]](#cite_note-42) Finally, Tonawanda Power Train originally opened in 1938 as a [Chevrolet](/wiki/Chevrolet "Chevrolet") engine plant and continues producing 4 and 8 cylinder engines in the Buffalo area.

Outside of the cities, agriculture has always been a major factor in the economy, especially [dairy farming](/wiki/Dairy_farming "Dairy farming").

[Ski country](/wiki/Ski_country "Ski country") runs through Western New York. [Glenwood](/wiki/Glenwood,_New_York "Glenwood, New York") and [Ellicottville](/wiki/Ellicottville_(town),_New_York "Ellicottville (town), New York") both have multiple ski resorts within their boundaries; Ellicottville, in particular, has seen massive [gentrification](/wiki/Gentrification "Gentrification") from the development of the large ski resorts within the town boundaries and has attempted to build on that growth by branding itself as a year-round destination with numerous festivals. At their peak in the 1960s, over a dozen ski resorts were in Western New York, most of them in Cattaraugus County.

In [Niagara County](/wiki/Niagara_County,_New_York "Niagara County, New York"), [viticulture](/wiki/Viticulture "Viticulture"), or wine culture, is also becoming a driver of the economy. In order to take advantage of this, the state has created the Niagara Wine Trail. To a certain extent, viticulture is also prominent in [Chautauqua County](/wiki/Chautauqua_County,_New_York "Chautauqua County, New York"), where [Welch's](/wiki/Welch%27s "Welch's") has a long history; the western Finger Lakes also have a wine industry.

American Rock Salt, the largest operating [salt mine](/wiki/Salt_mine "Salt mine") in the United States, is located in Livingston County.[[39]](#cite_note-43)

During the late 1990s, then-Governor [George Pataki](/wiki/George_Pataki "George Pataki") signed an agreement granting the [Seneca Nation of New York](/wiki/Seneca_Nation_of_New_York "Seneca Nation of New York") the right to acquire and build three full-scale [casino](/wiki/Casino "Casino") gaming operations. The three casinos ([Seneca Niagara Casino](/wiki/Seneca_Niagara_Casino "Seneca Niagara Casino") in Niagara Falls, [Seneca Allegany Casino](/wiki/Seneca_Allegany_Casino "Seneca Allegany Casino") in Salamanca and the controversial [Seneca Buffalo Creek Casino](/wiki/Seneca_Buffalo_Creek_Casino "Seneca Buffalo Creek Casino") in Buffalo) are the only casinos that operate over all of Western New York as part of the agreement, for which the Senecas pay a portion of revenues to the state of New York (which, in turn, returns part of that money to the host municipalities). Twice over the course of the compact's history have the Senecas stopped paying their exclusivity payments: once in a dispute over whether [video lottery terminals](/wiki/Video_lottery_terminal "Video lottery terminal") constituted the breach of the agreement, and again in 2017 after the Senecas claimed they are no longer required to pay the exclusivity payments but still maintain exclusivity under the language of the agreement.

The three native tribes in the region (Seneca Nation, [Tonawanda Band](/wiki/Tonawanda_Band_of_Seneca "Tonawanda Band of Seneca") and [Tuscarora](/wiki/Tuscarora_Reservation "Tuscarora Reservation")) also have a large influence in the [convenience store](/wiki/Convenience_store "Convenience store") sector. The tribes have long maintained that they are exempt from state [excise taxes](/wiki/Excise_tax "Excise tax") on gasoline and tobacco products, which allows native-owned convenience stores to provide lower prices than their non-native counterparts. Gasoline tax advantages were most pronounced in the 1990s, when low overall gas prices made the tax advantage far more noticeable. In the 1990s and 2000s, a mail-order tobacco industry thrived on Seneca reservations, an industry that has mostly been cut off since then. Increasing regulations now prohibit Senecas from receiving or selling name-brand cigarettes without taxes, sending tobacco products by mail ([Prevent All Cigarette Trafficking Act](/w/index.php?title=Prevent_All_Cigarette_Trafficking_Act&action=edit&redlink=1 "Prevent All Cigarette Trafficking Act (page does not exist)")) or private delivery, or accepting credit cards for remote purchases; the Senecas still sell native-produced tobacco products in brick-and-mortar stores on the reservations. Large tobacco farms can be seen in much of the [Cattaraugus Reservation](/wiki/Cattaraugus_Reservation,_Erie_County,_New_York "Cattaraugus Reservation, Erie County, New York") to meet the demand for native-grown, tax-exempt tobacco.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/50/Niagara_falls_panorama.jpg/960px-Niagara_falls_panorama.jpg)](/wiki/File:Niagara_falls_panorama.jpg)

Panoramic view of [Niagara Falls](/wiki/Niagara_Falls "Niagara Falls") from the Canadian side

Western New York is home to two significant scenic attractions. [Niagara Falls](/wiki/Niagara_Falls "Niagara Falls") is undoubtedly the most famous attraction in Western New York. Forming part of Western New York's border with Canada, the Falls has evolved into a major destination for tourists and locals alike.

Another, less famous scenic attraction, is [Letchworth State Park](/wiki/Letchworth_State_Park "Letchworth State Park"). Located 35 miles south of Rochester, Letchworth State Park has been termed "The Grand Canyon of the East".

[Allegany State Park](/wiki/Allegany_State_Park "Allegany State Park") in southern Cattaraugus County offers 65,000 acres of recreation space for camping, hiking, cabin rentals, swimming, and (during the winter) snowmobiling and cross-country skiing. It is mostly surrounded by the [Allegany Indian Reservation](/wiki/Allegany_Indian_Reservation "Allegany Indian Reservation") and to the south by the [Allegheny National Forest](/wiki/Allegheny_National_Forest "Allegheny National Forest").

Western New York is also known as the home of the [Chautauqua Institution](/wiki/Chautauqua_Institution "Chautauqua Institution") near [Jamestown, New York](/wiki/Jamestown,_New_York "Jamestown, New York"). Jamestown is also home to the Lucille Ball-Desi Arnaz Museum.

A small, but important, historic site is the [Holland Land Office](/wiki/Holland_Land_Office "Holland Land Office") in [Batavia, New York](/wiki/Batavia,_New_York "Batavia, New York").

Many towns along a nationally important historic site, the [Erie Canal](/wiki/Erie_Canal "Erie Canal"), have lovely waterfront parks and bike trails.

The [Genesee Country Village and Museum](/wiki/Genesee_Country_Village_and_Museum "Genesee Country Village and Museum") in [Mumford, New York](/wiki/Mumford,_New_York "Mumford, New York") is a well-known living history museum.

The Rolling Hills Asylum; located in [East Bethany, New York](/wiki/East_Bethany,_New_York "East Bethany, New York"). Regarded as one of the most haunted places in New York and included in various paranormal shows like Ghost Asylum, Destination Fear and many others.

The [George Eastman House](/wiki/George_Eastman_House "George Eastman House") is in Rochester, and is the world's oldest photography museum.

The [Theodore Roosevelt Inaugural National Historic Site](/wiki/Theodore_Roosevelt_Inaugural_National_Historic_Site "Theodore Roosevelt Inaugural National Historic Site"), known locally as the Wilcox Mansion, is in Buffalo. It is the site of the first presidential inauguration of [Theodore Roosevelt](/wiki/Theodore_Roosevelt "Theodore Roosevelt") following the assassination of President [William McKinley](/wiki/William_McKinley "William McKinley") who had been attending the [Pan-American Exposition](/wiki/Pan-American_Exposition "Pan-American Exposition") in Buffalo in 1901.

[Darien Lake](/wiki/Darien_Lake "Darien Lake"), a theme park and concert venue in Genesee County, is also a popular summertime destination, drawing in crowds from all over Western and Central New York, as well as from southern Ontario.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] It has benefited from its location about halfway between Buffalo and Rochester and is one of the region's most successful concert venues.

## Sports

Main article: [Sports in Western New York](/wiki/Sports_in_Western_New_York "Sports in Western New York")

### Major professional teams

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_New_York&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Major professional teams")]

Western New York is represented by two major league sports teams: the [Buffalo Bills](/wiki/Buffalo_Bills "Buffalo Bills") of the [NFL](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") and the [Buffalo Sabres](/wiki/Buffalo_Sabres "Buffalo Sabres") of the [NHL](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League"). The Bills play their home football games at [Highmark Stadium](/wiki/Ralph_Wilson_Stadium "Ralph Wilson Stadium"), the largest sports stadium in all of New York State, located just south of the city of Buffalo in [Orchard Park](/wiki/Orchard_Park_(town),_New_York "Orchard Park (town), New York"). The Bills are the only team to reach the [Super Bowl](/wiki/Super_Bowl "Super Bowl") in four consecutive seasons (1990–1993) in NFL history.

The [Buffalo Sabres](/wiki/Buffalo_Sabres "Buffalo Sabres") also have a strong following in Western New York, and are consistently ranked among the highest-rated local TV hockey markets and the highest-rated markets for NBC/NBCSN regular season games.[[40]](#cite_note-44)[[41]](#cite_note-45)[[42]](#cite_note-46) Primarily because of the strong regional interest in the team, in 2016 [MSG Western New York](/wiki/MSG_Western_New_York "MSG Western New York") was launched. The [regional sports network](/wiki/Regional_sports_network "Regional sports network") is a sub-feed of the [MSG Network](/wiki/MSG_(TV_network) "MSG (TV network)"), but is tilted towards Western New York sports and controlled by [Pegula Sports and Entertainment](/wiki/Pegula_Sports_and_Entertainment "Pegula Sports and Entertainment"). The Sabres reached the [Stanley Cup Final](/wiki/Stanley_Cup_Final "Stanley Cup Final") in [1975](/wiki/1975_Stanley_Cup_Final "1975 Stanley Cup Final") and [1999](/wiki/1999_Stanley_Cup_Final "1999 Stanley Cup Final"). The [Toronto Maple Leafs](/wiki/Toronto_Maple_Leafs "Toronto Maple Leafs") and [Ottawa Senators](/wiki/Ottawa_Senators "Ottawa Senators") are the Sabres' biggest rivals. On January 1, 2008, the Sabres played the [Pittsburgh Penguins](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Penguins "Pittsburgh Penguins") in the inaugural [Winter Classic](/wiki/Winter_Classic "Winter Classic") at Ralph Wilson Stadium (now Highmark Stadium). This was the first ever regular-season NHL game held outdoors to be played in the United States.[[43]](#cite_note-47)

Rochester is the 10th largest metropolitan area in the United States to not include a major professional team.[[44]](#cite_note-48)

#### Baseball

#### Soccer

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_New_York&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Soccer")]

Western New York currently does not have a team in [Major League Soccer](/wiki/Major_League_Soccer "Major League Soccer") (occasionally considered the fifth major league on the American sporting landscape), although Rochester had been mentioned in the past as a candidate for a new expansion franchise and previously hosted the [Rochester Lancers](/wiki/Rochester_Lancers_(1967%E2%80%9380) "Rochester Lancers (1967–80)") in MLS's predecessor, the [NASL](/wiki/North_American_Soccer_League_(1968%E2%80%9384) "North American Soccer League (1968–84)"). The speculation of Rochester getting an MLS team was due to the relative success of the [Rochester Rhinos](/wiki/Rochester_Rhinos "Rochester Rhinos"). However, the Rhinos later a downturn in attendance due to a number of factors leading to the team going dormant in 2018 and re-emerging as Rochester, NY FC in a lower division of soccer in 2022.[[49]](#cite_note-53) Such speculation has also decreased in the 21st century, in part because of MLS's refusal to put teams in metropolitan areas without any other major league teams and greatly increased competition for MLS expansion franchises. The former Rhinos franchise are best known as being the only non-MLS team to win the [US Open Cup](/wiki/US_Open_Cup "US Open Cup") since that competition was opened to MLS teams.

Western New York was represented in [National Women's Soccer League](/wiki/National_Women%27s_Soccer_League "National Women's Soccer League") by the [Western New York Flash](/wiki/Western_New_York_Flash "Western New York Flash"), a team that originally began play in the W-league in 2008. The Flash, like the Rhinos, played its home games at [Sahlen's Stadium](/wiki/Marina_Auto_Stadium "Marina Auto Stadium") in Rochester but were based out of suburban Buffalo. U.S. soccer star [Abby Wambach](/wiki/Abby_Wambach "Abby Wambach") was a member of the Flash. In 2017 the team transferred its NWSL rights to North Carolina and self-relegated to an amateur team. The Western New York region is home to [FC Buffalo](/wiki/FC_Buffalo "FC Buffalo") and the current incarnation of the [Rochester Lancers](/wiki/Rochester_Lancers_(2015) "Rochester Lancers (2015)"), both of whom compete in the [NPSL](/wiki/National_Premier_Soccer_League "National Premier Soccer League").

No [indoor soccer](/wiki/Indoor_soccer "Indoor soccer") teams currently play in the region. The region had previously been represented by the [Buffalo Stallions](/wiki/Buffalo_Stallions "Buffalo Stallions"), [Buffalo Blizzard](/wiki/Buffalo_Blizzard "Buffalo Blizzard") and another [Rochester Lancers](/wiki/Rochester_Lancers_(2011%E2%80%9315) "Rochester Lancers (2011–15)").

A number of Motorsports tracks operate in Western New York. The most prominent of these is [Holland Speedway](/wiki/Holland_Speedway "Holland Speedway"), a [NASCAR](/wiki/NASCAR "NASCAR")-sanctioned track (currently hosting races in the [Whelen All-American Series](/wiki/Whelen_All-American_Series "Whelen All-American Series")). Other, generally [dirt](/wiki/Dirt_track_racing "Dirt track racing"), tracks include [Stateline Speedway](/wiki/Stateline_Speedway "Stateline Speedway") in [Busti](/wiki/Busti,_New_York "Busti, New York"), [Little Valley Speedway](/wiki/Little_Valley_Speedway "Little Valley Speedway") in [Little Valley](/wiki/Little_Valley_(village),_New_York "Little Valley (village), New York"), [Freedom Raceway](/wiki/Freedom_Motorsports_Park "Freedom Motorsports Park") in [Freedom](/wiki/Freedom,_New_York "Freedom, New York"), Chapel Hill Raceway in [Humphrey](/wiki/Humphrey,_New_York "Humphrey, New York"), [Ransomville Speedway](/wiki/Ransomville_Speedway "Ransomville Speedway") in [Ransomville](/wiki/Ransomville,_New_York "Ransomville, New York") and [Canandaigua Motorsports Park](/wiki/Land_of_Legends_Raceway "Land of Legends Raceway") in [Canandaigua](/wiki/Canandaigua,_New_York "Canandaigua, New York"). The last of these is the location in which NASCAR driver [Tony Stewart](/wiki/Tony_Stewart "Tony Stewart") was involved in a fatal accident with fellow racer Kevin Ward Jr. in 2014 during a [sprint car](/wiki/Sprint_car_racing "Sprint car racing") race.

[College hockey](/wiki/College_hockey "College hockey") also has a following, with [Canisius College](/wiki/Canisius_College "Canisius College"), [Niagara University](/wiki/Niagara_University "Niagara University"), and [Rochester Institute of Technology](/wiki/Rochester_Institute_of_Technology "Rochester Institute of Technology") competing at the [Division I](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I "NCAA Division I") level and several other teams (including most of the SUNY schools) competing in [Division III](/wiki/NCAA_Division_III "NCAA Division III") and/or the [ACHA](/wiki/American_Collegiate_Hockey_Association "American Collegiate Hockey Association").

[College basketball](/wiki/College_basketball "College basketball") has several teams in the area. The four teams in Division I are known as the "Big 4" and include the Canisius Golden Griffins, [Niagara Purple Eagles](/wiki/Niagara_Purple_Eagles_men%27s_basketball "Niagara Purple Eagles men's basketball"), [UB Bulls](/wiki/Buffalo_Bulls_men%27s_basketball "Buffalo Bulls men's basketball"), and [St. Bonaventure Bonnies](/wiki/St._Bonaventure_Bonnies_men%27s_basketball "St. Bonaventure Bonnies men's basketball"). Prior to UB's ascension to Division I, the three other schools—all [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") institutions—were known as the [Little Three](/wiki/Western_New_York_Little_Three_Conference "Western New York Little Three Conference"). In addition to the men's teams, the [women's basketball](/wiki/Women%27s_basketball "Women's basketball") teams of [UB](/wiki/Buffalo_Bulls_women%27s_basketball "Buffalo Bulls women's basketball") and [St. Bonaventure](/wiki/St._Bonaventure_Bonnies_women%27s_basketball "St. Bonaventure Bonnies women's basketball") have earned national success in the 2010s.

[College football](/wiki/College_football "College football") is much more sparse; only one team competes at the Division I Bowl Subdivision, the State University of New York at Buffalo's [UB Bulls](/wiki/Buffalo_Bulls_football "Buffalo Bulls football"). [Erie Community College](/wiki/Erie_Community_College "Erie Community College") fields a team in junior college football known as the Kats, while Buffalo State, Brockport State, The [University of Rochester](/wiki/University_of_Rochester "University of Rochester"), [St. John Fisher College](/wiki/St._John_Fisher_College "St. John Fisher College"), Alfred State and Alfred University field teams at the Division III level.

Greater Rochester is the largest metropolitan area in the United States to not include a college or university with a full [NCAA Division I](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I "NCAA Division I") athletic program. [Rochester Institute of Technology](/wiki/Rochester_Institute_of_Technology "Rochester Institute of Technology") competes in Division I only in men's and women's ice hockey, and therefore cannot offer [athletic scholarships](/wiki/Athletic_scholarships "Athletic scholarships").

Public high school athletic teams compete in sections 5 (Rochester area) and 6 (Buffalo area) of the [New York State Public High School Athletic Association](/wiki/New_York_State_Public_High_School_Athletic_Association "New York State Public High School Athletic Association"). Western New York Roman Catholic high schools compete in the [Monsignor Martin Athletic Association](/wiki/Monsignor_Martin_Athletic_Association "Monsignor Martin Athletic Association").
